So a quick summary.
Basically, my career are on those two big pillars, which is as a founder and as a community manager, both super interconnected.

My first job is from Endeavor, which is an organization that supports entrepreneurs working as community managers.

I ran it as Cross.
I passed into experiences like Airbnb and WeWork, building community, typically launching the operation or a new program in Latin, and have these two experiences as a founder.
My first one, which was between Endeavor and Airbnb, which was super building kind of door dash for vegan food in Sao Paulo, super bootstrap, super a junior person building something.
And it was very simple experience, but super relevant.
And for me to have this understandment how it looks like, start something, put in the market, have some traction.

And then my last experience that that time took five years and which was something bigger, which is when I built this company called Afpass, which we built a huge community around of software engineers, transform that, raise some capital, transform that in a SaaS platform, build a team, have good names in our portfolio.
And eventually shut down the company, which is a huge chapter in my life.

And then I'm here looking for working in an environment that provides me at some level that feeling of starting from scratch, building something, autonomy, and at the same time working in, I'll say, less stressful than build my own company.
So Series A company looks like a company.

So, yeah, that's the overall summary.
That's awesome.
And maybe I'll do just a quick, like, I love, I'm sure you, like, Jason already gave you some of the context, right?
Like, but, so, we are building the company in a very different way.

Most companies, they would fundraise and then build something and then figure out a way to go to market and then get some early traction and then iterate on the product and then raise more money, keep building, and kind of follow that pattern.
And we took a very different way, which was, I, we started as, as just a service and, and then over time.

The intent initially wasn't to build this crazy platform, was just there's a lot of demand for the service, which is helping companies create content that helped them grow and gain visibility and whatnot.
And I just wanted to use AI to find ways to streamline that.
But then very quickly, there was just overwhelming demand, and we just started to scale.
What are some ways we can automate, use AI, and really quickly figure that all the tools out there weren't doing what we needed to.
So we started building our own platform to power our services and quickly realized the much bigger opportunity.
And then when we fundraise, so we were bootstrapped, and when we fundraise in February, we were a little over $4 million in revenue and run rate.
And now we're on track to hit $15 million this year.
So it's been awesome.
But also, it's very different because we, like community was part of our journey from the beginning, right?
Like I started doing these workshops last April, and we had 33 people came and paid 500 bucks to join them.
It was just me teaching them how to build workflows and how to think about content for five hours.
And then we did three more of those workshops, and that's how we got our first customers.
And so it was always like very community driven.
And I think now more than ever, community and distribution can truly be more of a moat because a lot of, like the tech is just very easy to kind of copy other people.
It's easy to, you know, like right now we have competitors that have raised a lot more money than we have that are literally targeting every single one of our customers.
The second we put a logo on our website, they hit up everybody there, right?
And so building these relationships is really.
But then also, my whole career, I just believe in companies should be more like publishers, and they should also, we should all act more like creators, right?
And so a lot of it kind of is coming to this, what we launched, which is AIledgrowth.com.
And the goal is to build in public, help everyone in the community, right?
Like using AI and growing with using AI, but then also be a place where it can fund itself.
So the more we grow it, the more we can invest back in it.

So it's almost like instead of doing marketing, what if we instead built this community as a product, and every dollar that comes in gets reinvested every month to make the community more valuable and keep growing it?
And I think pretty quickly, this could be a massive operation in and of itself, right?
Which is pretty exciting.
so what I'm trying to prove it out is like, can you have this be where we recruit from?

Program in scale, because at that first time, we spent a huge time to have this first 100 people.

think by the end of the first...

it.

That's what means success at that point.

And the cool stuff, and for me, it was one of those moments that...

I lead that front.

So essentially, we start to be part of some local communities from startups, some specific forums for CEOs and CFOs to bring the topic about, hey, how it looks like to be more efficient in the workplace and office material, how it looks like to work.
Together in the same place with other big startups and VCs all together in the same place.
And we start to join some events, talk, bring some people for meetups, building our own community, circling around these topics about the future of work, the work-life balance.
So HR started to join the conversation, CFOs in, finally started to join the conversation about how efficient it's working and co-working.
Founders started to join our conversation, saying, hey, we have to build this.
It's an offline social network, it's amazing, all together.
So we built that whole excitement about the product prior to launch.
And we were able to launch that about over 80%.
And it was amazing.
It was a great experience at that time, built communities since day one.
And when the product already launched, it became way cooler because now I can have even metrics to connect.
We really, my team have to connect at least one, two companies per week, and then follow up if something works on that.

It was a very good time in those first two years.

Okay, so tell me more, like, I like this concept of connections, because one of the communities I launched when I was at Scale AI was called Exchange.
And one of the things that we never really got it operationalized was the curated introductions and connections.
So really curious, like, on that front, like, how did the idea first come about?
Yeah, for us, of course, it's not an easy thing to metric in terms of success of connection.
But we tried to make, there is two reasons for doing that.
One is, since they won, which...
Try to differentiate ourselves as a co-working space.
No, we are business-driven here.
We're not focused on hospitality and operations only.
We're business-driven.
All the teams are people coming from this background of startup and tech.
And we try to make sure that there's this feeling of, here, I have more opportunities.
So if once a week the team introduced me for other potential client or vendor or whatever, this is generate extra value for me.
So we try to metric that in number of connections that make sense.
And after three months, we're starting to tracking how it's if it makes sense, if you have any follow-up meeting after that, some business were generated.
And, of course, it's easiest for us.
Because we are dead.
We spent the whole day with all the companies.
So it's easy to track that, to just knock on the door and, hey, how was that conversation?
So that helps a lot.
Of course, online, this would be a little bit more complicated.
But yeah, I think it was a very important way to not just generate value, but of course, make us to understand more about our members.
So because to make this connection, we have to understand a lot.
Yeah.
How was it run?
So it was like whoever was managing that location, where did they track?
What did they track?
In other words, if I wanted to replicate this program for us, give me the quick version of how you did it, essentially.
Yeah.
Basically, we structured the entire portfolio of the building with some by industry, size of the company.
We already made our homework to understand deeply what...
Every company are doing there, and how can we aggrupate that?
The second...
That was like a spreadsheet, or where did that live?
A spreadsheet.
It was a spreadsheet.
And basically there we can understand that, okay, we have these startups that are looking for new customers and capital.
We have these big companies that a lot, they love to talk about best practice of HR person and all that stuff.
There is this people that are basically service providers, there is lawyers and accountants.
So we create these layers, and we permanently are talking with these members.
Basically, bi-weekly basis, we're talking about five, with five, ten of those members.
And just to follow up about the moment of the company, what are the biggest challenges right now?
How can we help you?
And based on that conversations, we started to align some connections.
Then we keep this rolling monthly basis.
So you have like the spreadsheet, right?
And you're constantly enriching what you know about the profile of the companies.
But then who and how do you determine like, I want to connect this company or this person in this company with that person in that company?
Like, how would you decide that?
Because I always found it really hard to operationalize stuff like that.
Yeah, yeah, it's not the most scalable thing.
But definitely, once is a kind of not that big universe, we're talking about, I know, maybe have 50, 40 companies in this universe in one location.
And of course, it's not only here, we can connect for other members for other locations.
But we own that.
And then we start to, with this conversation, we start to understand some demands.
For example, if we talk about one service provider, they say, hey, I would love to have opportunity to talk with the HR person.
